justification: Emigration intention is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning willingness, desire, and perceived likelihood of moving—no single item captures the full motivational spectrum. All three questions tap the same latent disposition toward emigration and, while their response formats differ slightly (binary-leaning vs. probability scale), they share an ordinal structure that makes mean-scoring defensible after rescaling to a common range before binning. Averaging reduces item-specific measurement error and produces a more reliable composite that is then binned into ≤5 ordered categories for the bridge model.

Perceived opportunity structure is a classic multi-faceted construct encompassing education, employment, entrepreneurship, credit access, and international mobility; no single domain adequately represents the broader structural perception. All five items share an identical 6-point Likert scale, making them directly combinable without rescaling, and their near-uniform high entropies (1.90–2.00 bits) indicate each contributes meaningful variance. A mean score averaged across all five items and binned into 5 ordered categories yields a parsimonious, high-reliability composite well-suited for the bridge regression.

Transnational social ties span distinct but correlated dimensions—having family abroad, having friends abroad, frequency of contact, and perceived support from abroad—that together define network embeddedness in the migration system. Items p21_1, p21_2, and p21_3 are excluded because they are follow-up filters (country destination, remittance receipt, remittance share) with low entropy and high missingness conditional on p21, which would distort a composite. The four retained items use heterogeneous but ordinal scales, so PCA on the first component is preferred over simple summation as it optimally weights each indicator by its shared variance and the resulting scores are binned into 5 quantile-based categories.

Attitudes toward foreigners are multi-dimensional, covering affective evaluations, economic attributions, perceived discrimination, and preferred policy stance; a single item would miss critical facets. Items p44 (behavioral contact, very low entropy 0.85) and p48 (rights opinions, low entropy 1.07 and more policy-preference than attitudinal) are excluded as weaker indicators, while the five retained items all have high entropy (1.77–2.04 bits) and span the evaluative, normative, and perceptual dimensions of the construct. Because the items use varying scale widths (4–7 categories), PCA on the first component is the most appropriate aggregation method, extracting the dominant attitudinal factor and binning it into 5 quantile categories for the bridge model.

The open-ended items p29a and p30aa_1, while high in entropy, are nominally coded categorical responses to free-text questions whose categories are not inherently ordered, making them unsuitable for ordinal aggregation or mean-scoring; p32_1 has low entropy (1.16 bits) and captures only job quality. The three retained items—perceived discrimination against Mexicans in the US, opinions on outcomes of emigrants, and perceived return migration since 2008—are all ordinally scaled, cover the evaluative cost-benefit perception space, and have adequate entropy (1.38–2.04 bits). Mean-scoring these three items and binning into ≤5 ordered categories provides a tractable, ordinal composite that balances content coverage with scale compatibility for the bridge regression.
